results.possibleIdentity: George H. Swan (1928–2015) was a communications engineer who spent the majority of his career at Bell Telephone Laboratories. Born in Brooklyn, New York, he served in the U.S. Marine Corps before attending the RCA Technical Institute, from which he graduated in 1950. He initially worked at Sperry Corporation (1950–1954) on military radar systems before joining Bell Laboratories, where he worked for 36 years until his retirement in 1990. At Bell Labs, he reached the rank of Distinguished Member of the Technical Staff. His technical contributions to communications engineering include research on microwave links and radio interference, notably his work titled "Interference Administration of Bell System Junction Stations at 4 GHz." Swan was a long-time resident of Red Bank, New Jersey, having moved there in 1966. In addition to his engineering career, he was an avid oil painter, which aligns with the profiles of engineers who collaborated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization during the late 1960s and 1970s.
